SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (key-value-predict attention improves perplexity; models mainly attend to the most recent ~5 tokens; a simple N-gram RNN matches attentive models) and provides an experimental setup and comparative results supporting them. The method equations (Sections 2.1–2.4) are coherent and consistent with standard attention/LSTM formulations, and the authors reimplemented relevant baselines and controlled for parameter counts. However, the paper does not report statistical significance tests, and some conclusions (inability to exploit long-range dependencies) could be confounded by training choices and dataset selection (e.g., BPTT length 20, specific Wikipedia subset), which are not fully analyzed. Overall the experiments substantiate the main claims but lack some robustness analyses and tests for alternative explanations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper adapts the known idea of separating key and value representations to language modeling and further proposes a triple split (key-value-predict), which is a natural extension but largely builds on prior work cited (Miller et al., Ba et al., Reed & de Freitas, Gulcehre). The observation that short attention spans suffice and the simple N-gram RNN baseline are useful empirical findings but are incremental rather than radically novel. The work clearly differentiates its contributions from related models (e.g., RMNs, HORNNs), but the core techniques are non-radical extensions of existing methods.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in a standard and logical way (introduction, methods with equations, related work, experiments, conclusion) and states its contributions early. Notation in the methods section is consistent and the equations (1–13) make the architectures easy to follow; experimental protocol is described with hyperparameters. The provided text contains some duplicated sentences and formatting artifacts (likely from the PDF to text conversion) and figures/tables are referenced but not shown here, which slightly hampers readability in this excerpt but does not obscure the main presentation.
